sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 11 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Only 1 cell changed at (63,57) - that's the timer bar at the bottom. The UP action didn't move the player. The player seems completely stuck. Let me reconsider the game mechanics.
Looking at the image again, I see:
- Left panel: white rectangle with a charcoal frame (containing white interior) and a green frame (containing white pixel - the player)
- Middle: purple vertical bar
- Right panel: white rectangle with a charcoal frame (containing white interior) and a green frame (containing black pixel - the target)
Wait, maybe I need to re-examine. The player started in the LEFT panel and moved right. Now it's stuck near the purple bar. But the target (black pixel) is in the RIGHT panel.
Maybe the game isn't about moving the player with arrow keys. Maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on something. Or maybe the arrow keys control something else.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the initial state:
- Left panel has a green frame with white center (player?) and a charcoal frame with light gray center
- Middle area has a green frame with black center (target?)
- Right panel has a charcoal frame with light gray center
After moving RIGHT several times, the player (white pixel in green frame) moved from the left panel toward the right. But now it's stuck.
Wait, let me look at the image more carefully. The green frame with the white pixel is now positioned just to the left of the purple bar. The green frame with the black pixel is to the right of the purple bar, in the right panel.
Maybe the goal is to get the player to the target's location. But the player can't move right anymore. Maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the target or some other object.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. Maybe the arrow keys don't move the player directly. Maybe they move some other element, or maybe they rotate something, or maybe they select something.
Let me look at what changed when I first pressed RIGHT. The player moved from (31,19) to (31,22), which is 3 cells right. The green frame also moved. But after several more RIGHT presses, the player only moved to (31,31) and then stopped.
Wait, I think I miscounted. Let me trace the player's position:
- Start: (31, 19)
- After 1 RIGHT: (31, 22) - moved 3 right
- After 3 more RIGHTs: still at (31, 22)? No, let me check.
Actually, looking at my earlier output:
After the first RIGHT: player at (31, 22)
After 3 more RIGHTs: player at (31, 28) 
After 5 more RIGHTs: player at (31, 31)
After 1 more RIGHT: player still at (31, 31) - stuck
So the player moved: 19 -> 22 -> 28 -> 31 -> stuck at 31.
The moves were: +3, +6, +3, +0. That's weird.
Wait, let me re-examine. After the first RIGHT action, I checked and found:
- White pixel at (31, 22)
- Green frame at [30,21]-[32,23]
Then after 3 more RIGHTs:
- White pixel at (31, 28)
- Green frame at [30,27]-[32,29]
Then after 5 more RIGHTs:
- White pixel at (31, 31)
- Green frame at [30,30]-[32,32]
Then after 1 more RIGHT:
- White pixel still at (31, 31)
- Green frame still at [30,30]-[32,32]
So the movement pattern is: 19 -> 22 (+3) -> 28 (+6) -> 31 (+3) -> stuck.
Hmm, the +6 jump is strange. Maybe some RIGHT presses didn't register or the batch executed differently.
Anyway, the player is now stuck at (31, 31). The target is at (31, 43). The player can't move right anymore.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the target or the charcoal frame. Or maybe I need to move LEFT first to reset, then try a different path.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the image again. I see the green frame with white pixel is now to the LEFT of the purple bar, not to the right. Let me re-examine the ASCII:
`gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNWNppppppwwwNBNwwwwwwwwwgg`
Breaking this down (starting from col 8):
- g at col 8
- wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww at cols 9-29
- NWN at cols 30-32 (player at col 31)
- pppppp at cols 33-38 (purple bar)
- www at cols 39-41
- NBN at cols 42-44 (target at col 43)
- wwwwwwww at cols 45-52
- gg at cols 53-54
So the player is at col 31, which is to the LEFT of the purple bar (cols 33-38). The target is at col 43, which is to the RIGHT of the purple bar.
The player can't move right past the purple bar. Maybe I need to find another way, like going around it using UP/DOWN, or maybe I need to use MOUSE to interact with something.
Let me try using MOUSE to click on the target (black pixel at 31, 43).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is stuck. Let me try MOUSE to click on the target (black pixel at 31, 43)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 43}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
